Re3 lueeting of September 7,8, 
L976
Consideration of repeal of Regulation
18700 (a) (I) (c)
Dear Corunissioners:
It is our understanding that the Fair Political-
Practices Commission (Commission) wil-1 consider repeali,ng
the referenced regulation during its September meeting.
This letter is written on behalf of the Irvine Unified
School District, 
Nevrport-Mesa Unified School District, 
Ocean
View School District, 
and orange Unified €chooL ffi
all of which are located i.n Orange County, California. 
Each
of these school districts supports repeal of Section I8700
(a) (1) (c).
The reason for our concern relates to parent
advisory committees for federally funded projects and whether
persons serving on these corunittees must be designated for
disclosure purposes uncler Local Conflict of Interest Codes.
At the present time Section 18700 (a) (1) (C) includes these
persons within the definition of public officiaL. 
The
reason why persons serving on parent advisory committees are
included is because their consideration is a legaJ- prereguisite
to receiving federal funds.
Each of these school districts is a recipient of
federal funds. FederaL funds are used for programs such as
early childhood education and bi lingual-bicultural programs.
5IO NEWPORT CENTER DRIVE 
SIJITE I22O
NEWPORT 8EACH, CALI FOF N IA 9?660
AREA COOE 7I4
IELEPHON E 644.93II
REF. OUR FILE
August 30, 1976 
\


.{LEXANDEII BOWIE
A LAvv Cc)R PC)FIATION
Fair Political Practices Commission
Auqust 30, 797 6
Page 2
One of the prerequisites for receiving federal
funding for funding of certain programs is a parent advisory
corunittee. The parent advisory committee must review the
school district's 
program prior to the time that it is
submitted to the federal government for purposes of being
considered for federal funding. Each school district 
must
specifically state that a parent advisory cornrnittee has
considered the program that is being subrnitted to the federal
government. Should a school districtrs particular program
be approved, tllen the parent advisory commitee will
advise the district 
regarding its implementation and effect
upon the corununity.
But for Section 18700 (a) (1) (C) , these parent
advisory committees are not public officials within the
meaning of Sectj-on 18700 (a). They do not make final
governmental decisions regarding the subnittal or implementation
of f ederally funded programs. These decisions are made by the
school board. A parent advisory comrnittee does not compel a
school board to submit a program. The role of the parent
advisory group for federally funded programs is restricted
to consultation and recornmendation. Its decisions are subject
to significant amendment or rnodification by the school
district.
A practi.cal reason for objecting to Section 18700
(a) (1) (c) is that it will discourage persons to serve on
parent advisory groups. I\Ie are certain that the Commission has
often heard this objection raised about various provisions of
the Political Reform Act of 1974. Nevertheless, persons are
selected for serving on parent advisory groups because these
persons come from the communj-ty that will be benefited by
the federally funded program-s. These persons serve on a
voluntary basis. Quite franl<Iy, the school districts 
are
concerned that disclosure reguirements will unduly burden
and dj,scourage persons from serving on parent advisory
groups. lievertheless, establishnent and use of such parent
advisory groups are legal prerequisites for receiving federal
funds under the Elementary anC Secondary Education Act and
similar federally funded prograns.


ALEXANDER EIOWIE
A LAW COFIPORATION
f'air Political Practices Commission
August 30, 197 6
Page 3
For the above stated. reasons, we believe that
Section 18700 (a) (1) (C) should be repealed. Your consideration
of this request is deeply apprecj-ated.
Very truly yours,
ALEXANDER BOWIE
A Lar", Corporation

sections.question_synthetic: Should the Fair Political Practices Commission repeal Regulation 18700(a)(1)(c), which currently requires persons serving on parent advisory committees for federally funded projects to be designated as public officials for disclosure purposes under local conflict of interest codes?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response/advice letter. The document lacks typical Q/C sections. A separate cover letter from FPPC dated September 2, 1976 indicates the request will be forwarded to the Commission. The sender argues parent advisory committee members should not be classified as public officials since they only provide consultation and recommendations, with final decisions remaining with the school board.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Should the Fair Political Practices Commission repeal Regulation 18700(a)(1)(c), which currently requires persons serving on parent advisory committees for federally funded projects to be designated as public officials for disclosure purposes under local conflict of interest codes?

embedding.summary: This document contains a letter from Alexander Bowie, A Law Corporation, dated August 30, 1976, on behalf of four Orange County school districts requesting that the FPPC repeal Section 18700(a)(1)(c) because it classifies parent advisory committee members as public officials, which they argue discourages voluntary participation despite these committees being legally required for federal funding.
